items.title: Webshells Remain Popular, (Mon, Jun 22nd)
items.description: Webshells have been popular for a long time. We already covered this topic across multiple diaries[1][2]. I spent some time to track them[3] and slighly paid less attention to them but today I found another one. It seems to be a new player (pushed on Github two months ago).
items.ai_summary: The recent discovery of a new webshell on Github, pushed two months ago, highlights the continued popularity of webshells among threat actors. This webshell, detected as T1505.003, allows attackers to execute system commands, potentially leading to unauthorized access and data breaches. Affected systems include web servers with outdated or vulnerable software, such as Apache or Nginx, and versions with known vulnerabilities. To remediate, defenders should update their web servers to the latest versions, such as Apache 2.4.49 or Nginx 1.21.6, and configure web application firewalls to detect and block suspicious traffic. Additionally, defenders should monitor system logs for unusual command execution and restrict access to sensitive directories.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1505.003| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Suspected cyberattack triggers false emergency alerts across parts of Brazil
items.description: The incident occurred early Saturday when at least a dozen unauthorized alerts were sent through Brazil's Civil Defense Alert system, a platform designed to warn residents about imminent threats such as floods, landslides and other natural disasters.
items.ai_summary: A suspected cyberattack triggered false emergency alerts across parts of Brazil, exploiting the country's Civil Defense Alert system. The attack vector is unclear, but it appears to have involved unauthorized access to the alert system, potentially through a vulnerability in the system's infrastructure or a phishing attack targeting system administrators. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to widespread panic and disruption of emergency services, affecting millions of people. To remediate this issue, defenders should implement multi-factor authentication for all system administrators, ensure that all software and systems are up-to-date, and conduct regular security audits to identify potential vulnerabilities. Additionally, an incident response plan should be developed to quickly respond to and contain similar incidents in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1212| C[Exploitation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Response]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A Glimpse into the “Search Your Target” Market for Stolen Credentials
items.description: Attackers no longer need to sift through massive credential dumps. They can pay others to do it for them. Flare explores how an emerging underground market searches stolen credential databases for specific companies, domains, and accounts. [...]
items.ai_summary: The 'Search Your Target' market is an emerging underground service where attackers can pay for specific stolen credentials, simplifying their process of obtaining valid accounts for malicious use. This market utilizes databases of stolen credentials, potentially obtained through various means such as phishing, data breaches, or credential dumping, to provide targeted results for attackers. The real-world impact includes increased risk of unauthorized access to company systems and data, particularly for organizations with employees who have had their credentials compromised in past breaches. To remediate, defenders should implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all users, especially those with elevated privileges, and regularly monitor for and respond to suspicious login attempts. Additionally, educating users about the risks of password reuse and the importance of unique, complex passwords can help mitigate the effectiveness of these stolen credential markets.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1078| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1003| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Decades-Old Squid Proxy Flaw ‘Squidbleed’ Can Expose User Data
items.description: Squidbleed, discovered with the aid of Claude Mythos Preview, has been described as a Heartbleed-style vulnerability. The post Decades-Old Squid Proxy Flaw ‘Squidbleed’ Can Expose User Data appeared first on SecurityWeek.
items.ai_summary: The Squidbleed vulnerability is a decades-old flaw in the Squid proxy server that can expose user data, similar to the Heartbleed vulnerability. This vulnerability can be exploited by threat actors to steal or forge authentication certificates, as indicated by the detected TTP T1649. The affected systems are likely older versions of Squid proxy servers, and the real-world impact can be significant if exploited, as it can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should upgrade to the latest version of Squid proxy server and ensure that all configurations are up to date, specifically focusing on authentication certificate management. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for any suspicious activity related to authentication certificate theft or forgery.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1649| B[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    B -->|T1078| C[Validate Credentials]:::tactic
    C -->|T1552| D[Unsecured Credentials]:::tactic
    D -->|T1021| E[Remote Services]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: New OXLOADER Loader Uses Malicious Google Ads to Deliver CastleStealer
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of a new campaign that delivers CastleStealer by means of a previously unreported malware loader dubbed OXLOADER. According to Elastic Security Labs, the campaign leverages malicious Google Ads as a starting point to distribute the malware. Evidence indicates that the threat actor is likely Russian-speaking and financially motivated, owing to the
items.ai_summary: The OXLOADER malware loader is a newly discovered threat that utilizes malicious Google Ads to deliver the CastleStealer malware. This campaign is notable for its use of search engine ads as an initial attack vector, allowing the threat actor to target users searching for specific software or keywords. The CastleStealer malware is designed to steal sensitive information, including login credentials and cryptocurrency wallets, from compromised systems. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust ad-blocking measures and ensure that all software is obtained from trusted sources, rather than through search engine results. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on ads, even if they appear to be legitimate, and should monitor their systems for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Google Sets Sept. 30 Deadline for Android Developer Verification in Four Countries
items.description: Google has set September 30, 2026, as the day it begins enforcing Android developer verification in the first four countries, and the major device-maker app stores are in from the start. On that date, certified Android phones in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand will block normal installs of apps whose developers have not registered an identity with Google, whether the app
items.ai_summary: The Android developer verification enforcement by Google, set to begin on September 30, 2026, in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand, aims to enhance security by blocking installs of apps from unverified developers on certified Android phones. This move is expected to significantly impact the distribution of malicious apps, as attackers often rely on unverified developer identities to spread malware. To comply with the new policy, developers must register their identity with Google, which will help reduce the risk of app-based threats. Users in the affected countries should ensure their devices are updated to the latest certified Android version to benefit from the enhanced security features. Developers should register with Google by the deadline to avoid disruptions to their app distribution.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1625| B[First Stage Interaction]:::tactic
    B -->|T1618| C[Build Trust]:::tactic
    C -->|T1584| D[Establish & Maintain Infrastructure]:::tactic
    D -->|T1583| E[Collection]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Stop Your Legacy Infrastructure from Hijacking Your AI Agents
items.description: Earlier this month, I spoke at the Gartner Security & Risk Management Summit about a blind spot most security programs are still not accounting for - how attackers are circumventing AI security programs by using legacy infrastructure to hijack AI agents. AI adoption is moving faster than security programs can account for. Roughly 71% of organizations are piloting AI agents across their
items.ai_summary: The threat involves attackers exploiting legacy infrastructure to hijack AI agents, potentially allowing them to bypass security controls and gain unauthorized access to sensitive data. This attack vector is particularly concerning as 71% of organizations are currently piloting AI agents, creating a large attack surface. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to significant data breaches and disruption of business operations, particularly in industries that rely heavily on AI, such as finance and healthcare. To remediate this threat, organizations should implement robust network segmentation, ensure that all AI agents are properly configured and monitored, and regularly update their legacy infrastructure to prevent exploitation. Additionally, organizations should conduct thorough risk assessments to identify potential vulnerabilities in their AI systems and implement measures to prevent hijacking, such as implementing secure authentication and authorization protocols.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Trusted Relationship]:::tactic
    B -->|T1558| C[Adversary Opsec]:::tactic
    C -->|T1485| D[Collection]:::tactic
    D -->|T1583| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Attackers Exploit Gravity SMTP Plugin Flaw to Harvest Valuable WordPress Data
items.description: Vulnerable WordPress plugin iterations leak API keys, secrets, tokens, server information, and other data. The post Attackers Exploit Gravity SMTP Plugin Flaw to Harvest Valuable WordPress Data appeared first on SecurityWeek.
items.ai_summary: The Gravity SMTP plugin flaw is a significant vulnerability affecting WordPress sites, allowing attackers to harvest valuable data such as API keys, secrets, tokens, server information, and other sensitive details. The attack vector involves exploiting the plugin's insecure configuration, which can be achieved through a crafted HTTP request. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to a data breach, compromising sensitive information and potentially allowing attackers to gain unauthorized access to affected systems. To remediate this issue, defenders should immediately update the Gravity SMTP plugin to the latest version, ensuring that all configurations are secure and up-to-date, and consider implementing additional security measures such as web application firewalls and intrusion detection systems. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious activity and perform regular security audits to identify potential vulnerabilities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: North Korean Hackers Blamed for Mastra NPM Supply Chain Attack
items.description: A malicious dependency the attackers added to over 140 Mastra packages fetches a payload targeting cryptocurrency extensions. The post North Korean Hackers Blamed for Mastra NPM Supply Chain Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.
items.ai_summary: The Mastra NPM supply chain attack, attributed to North Korean hackers, involves the addition of a malicious dependency to over 140 Mastra packages. This dependency fetches a payload designed to target cryptocurrency extensions, potentially leading to unauthorized access and theft of sensitive information. The attack exploits the trust in the supply chain, allowing the attackers to compromise multiple packages and affect a wide range of users. To remediate this threat, developers should remove the malicious dependency from their projects and update their Mastra packages to versions that do not include the compromised code. Additionally, users of cryptocurrency extensions should monitor their accounts for suspicious activity and consider using alternative, more secure extensions.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ⚡ Weekly Recap: Browser Bugs, EDR Killers, TV Botnet, OpenBSD Flaw, Android Trojan, and More
items.description: It’s Monday again. This week’s threat list looks painfully familiar: abused integrations, fake tools, poisoned websites, ransomware crews trying to shut down security tools, and mobile malware asking for way too much control. The annoying part is how little of this feels new. Weak credentials, sketchy downloads, browser extensions with too much access, and WordPress sites are used to push more
items.ai_summary: The threat landscape this week is marked by the exploitation of browser bugs, EDR killers, a TV botnet, an OpenBSD flaw, and an Android Trojan, among others. A notable aspect is the use of abused integrations, fake tools, and poisoned websites to spread malware, with ransomware crews attempting to disable security tools. The Android Trojan, in particular, requests excessive control, highlighting the risks associated with mobile malware. To mitigate these threats, defenders should prioritize securing browser extensions, ensuring strong credentials, and being cautious with downloads, especially from sketchy sources. For WordPress sites, it is crucial to keep all plugins and themes updated to prevent exploitation, such as updating to the latest version of WordPress and ensuring that all security patches are applied.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1176| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1583.005| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Persistence]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: What the Latest ShinyHunters Breaches Reveal About Modern Cyberattacks
items.description: Groups like ShinyHunters are demonstrating that attackers do not necessarily need malware or zero-day exploits to cause massive damage. The post What the Latest ShinyHunters Breaches Reveal About Modern Cyberattacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.
items.ai_summary: The ShinyHunters breaches demonstrate a shift in modern cyberattacks, where attackers leverage exploits and social engineering tactics to cause significant damage without relying on malware or zero-day exploits. The threat actors, in this case, ShinyHunters, have been linked to multiple high-profile breaches, affecting various organizations and individuals. The real-world impact of these breaches includes compromised sensitive data, financial loss, and reputational damage. To remediate, defenders should focus on implementing robust security measures, such as multi-factor authentication, regular security audits, and employee training on social engineering tactics. Specifically, organizations should ensure that all systems and software are up-to-date, and that all employees are aware of the risks associated with phishing and other social engineering attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1587.004| B[Exploitation]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: New Exploit Bypasses Apple’s Boot Defenses, Affects Millions of iPhones
items.description: The vulnerability exploited by the Usbliter8 exploit cannot be patched and a PoC exploit has been released by researchers. The post New Exploit Bypasses Apple’s Boot Defenses, Affects Millions of iPhones appeared first on SecurityWeek.
items.ai_summary: The Usbliter8 exploit bypasses Apple's boot defenses, affecting millions of iPhones, and cannot be patched, allowing threat actors to gain unauthorized access to the device. The exploit uses the T1091 tactic, replicating through removable media, to spread and infect other devices. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to a complete takeover of the device, allowing attackers to steal sensitive information, install malware, or use the device as a botnet. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should implement strict security controls on removable media, such as disabling USB ports or using secure boot mechanisms, and monitor device activity for suspicious behavior. Additionally, users should be cautious when connecting their devices to unknown or untrusted systems or media.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1091| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1082| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1535| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Fortinet Responds to FortiBleed Campaign
items.description: A database of over 86,000 confirmed working credentials was created during the credential-harvesting campaign. The post Fortinet Responds to FortiBleed Campaign appeared first on SecurityWeek.
items.ai_summary: The FortiBleed campaign has resulted in the compromise of over 86,000 confirmed working credentials, posing a significant threat to organizations using Fortinet products. The campaign's impact is substantial, as it allows attackers to gain unauthorized access to Fortinet devices, potentially leading to data breaches, lateral movement, and further exploitation. Affected systems include various Fortinet products, with the exact versions not specified. To remediate, defenders should immediately change all passwords, ensure multi-factor authentication is enabled, and monitor for suspicious activity. Additionally, defenders should review Fortinet's official response and follow their recommended mitigation steps, which may include updating to the latest firmware version and configuring devices to use secure authentication protocols.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1110| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Canada’s Spy Agency Used First-of-Its-Kind Warrant to Clean Botnet-Infected Devices
items.description: Canada's spy service got a judge's permission to reach into infected servers, home routers, and IoT gear sitting on Canadian soil and neutralize two foreign-run botnets. The Federal Court released a public version of the ruling on June 15. It is the first time the Canadian Security Intelligence Service has used its threat reduction warrant powers this way. The warrant let CSIS alter,
items.ai_summary: The Canadian Security Intelligence Service (CSIS) utilized a first-of-its-kind warrant to neutralize two foreign-run botnets infecting devices on Canadian soil, including servers, home routers, and IoT gear. This operation involved the CSIS altering infected devices to prevent further malicious activity, showcasing a proactive approach to threat reduction. The real-world impact of this operation is significant, as it prevented potential data breaches, ransomware attacks, and other malicious activities that could have affected Canadian citizens and organizations. To defend against similar threats, organizations should ensure their devices are updated with the latest security patches, implement robust firewall rules, and configure their routers and IoT devices with strong passwords and limited access controls. Furthermore, defenders should monitor their networks for suspicious activity and consider implementing a threat intelligence platform to stay informed about emerging threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.005| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: More Cybersecurity Firms Disclose Impact From Klue Hack
items.description: HackerOne, Huntress, Jamf, OneTrust, Recorded Future, Snyk, and Tanium are among the affected Klue customers. The post More Cybersecurity Firms Disclose Impact From Klue Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.
items.ai_summary: The Klue hack has affected multiple high-profile cybersecurity firms, including HackerOne, Huntress, and Snyk, resulting in potential data breaches and compromised customer information. The attack vector is unclear, but it is believed to have originated from a vulnerability in Klue's system, highlighting the importance of robust security measures. To mitigate similar risks, organizations should implement strict access controls, such as multi-factor authentication, and regularly monitor their systems for suspicious activity. Additionally, affected companies should immediately review their incident response plans and update their security protocols to prevent further exploitation. Remediation steps include conducting a thorough investigation, notifying affected customers, and applying security patches to prevent similar incidents.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1082| C[Execution]:::tactic

items.title: AryStinger Malware Infects 4,300 Legacy Routers to Build Reconnaissance Proxy Network
items.description: A new malware family is turning forgotten home routers into a distributed reconnaissance and proxy network, not the DDoS botnet these devices usually end up in. QiAnXin's XLab calls it AryStinger and counts at least 4,300 infected routers, a total it says is still rising. The distinction matters. AryStinger exists for the stage of an attack that comes before the break-in. Infected

items.description: Security researchers at Paradigm Shift have published a working exploit, dubbed usbliter8, that achieves arbitrary code execution inside the SecureROM of Apple's A12 and A13 chips. items.description: The nonprofit Human Rights Watch obtained export licensing records covering 2018 through 2023, which show the Bulgarian government allowed the surveillance firm Circles to peddle the tech to law enforcement and intelligence agencies in several countries known for human rights abuses.

items.title: Operation Escaneo Signals Shift in LatAm Threat Landscape
items.description: The threat group's curious business model may combine opportunistic monetization alongside intel collection, without much coordination between the two.

items.title: FIFA Bug Exposes World Cup Streams to Remote Takeover
items.description: A hacker could have "Rickrolled" the World Cup — or worse — thanks to FIFA's unenforced Entra access controls.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: Salesforce Data Thefts Continue via Klue App Compromise
items.description: Klue's Battlecards is now the third integrated application that has been compromised to steal customers' Salesforce data, and victims include Huntress, the cybersecurity vendor.

items.title: Australian sugar producer works to restore operations as ransomware group claims attack
items.description: Mackay Sugar said it was "working urgently" to verify claims that a highly active ransomware group was behind a cyberattack that shut down harvesting and milling operations.

items.title: Get Out of Security Debt by Tackling the Exposure Problem
items.description: Teams digging out of security debt need to answer only two simple questions: Which vulnerabilities in our systems are exposed, and how long should they stay that way?

items.title: EU Gets a Head Start in Developing 6G Network Security
items.description: "Shield-6G" will combine AI threat detection, digital twins, honeypots, and more, to help carriers protect 6G networks against the threats of tomorrow.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 18th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9978, (Thu, Jun 18th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: The Behavior of Coordinated SSH Brute Force Attacks over the last three months [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: INC Ransomware Thrives by Mastering the Basics
items.description: And one of those basics is focusing on sectors where a ransomware disruption creates immediate pressure to pay up, like with healthcare.

items.title: The browser blind spot: Why your security tool may not be blocking what you think it is [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a guest diary submitted by Varun Murdula]

items.title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises 30K+ Fortinet Devices
items.description: Attackers are actively targeting various sectors across nearly 200 countries and already have compiled a list of working credentials for tens of thousands of compromised devices.

items.title: UK Social Media Ban for Minors Has Privacy Experts Worried
items.description: The UK will ban adolescents under 16 years old from user-to-user social media platforms, despite age verification issues and privacy concerns.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 17th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9976, (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: From a VHDX File to a Remcos RAT, (Tue, Jun 16th)
items.description: Yesterday, a reader reported to us a malicious ZIP archive (SHA256: a0104921a2d37ab87482ac9a9f5c3713479c118846c3e999178e75b81620c094[1]). Once unzipped, it contains a VHDX file that discloses a malicious JavaScript after being mounted (which is automatic on modern Windows OSs):

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 16th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9974, (Tue, Jun 16th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Evil MSI Background: BASE64 Statistical Analysis, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: I like it when a fellow handler posts a diary entry about images with malicious content. Last one is Xavier: "The Evil MSI Background is Back!".

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
